message.group-title: Endocrinology (including Diabetes Mellitus and Metabolic Disease)
message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>AIMS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To compare trends in diagnoses, monitoring and mortality in patients with type 2 diabetes, before and after the first COVID-19 peak.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>METHODS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We constructed a cohort of 25 million patients using electronic health records from 1831 UK general practices registered with the Clinical Practice Research Datalink (CPRD), including 14 million patients followed between March and December 2020. We compared trends using regression models and 10-year historical data. We extrapolated the number of missed/delayed diagnoses using UK Office for National Statistics data.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>RESULTS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In England, rates of new type 2 diabetes diagnoses were reduced by 70% (95% CI 68%-71%) in April 2020, with similar reductions in Northern Ireland, Scotland and Wales. Between March and December, we estimate that there were approximately 60,000 missed/delayed diagnoses across the UK. In April, rates of HbA
                    <jats:sub>1c</jats:sub>
                    testing were greatly reduced in England (reduction: 77% (95% CI 76%-78%)) with more marked reductions in the other UK nations (83% (83-84%)). Reduced rates of diagnosing and monitoring were particularly evident in older people, in males, and in those from deprived areas. In April, the mortality rate in England was more than 2-fold higher (112%) compared to prior trends, but was only 65% higher in Northern Ireland, Scotland and Wales.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>CONCLUSIONS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>As engagement increases, healthcare services will need to manage the backlog and anticipate greater deterioration of glucose control due to delayed diagnoses and reduced monitoring in those with pre-existing diabetes. Older people, men, and those from deprived backgrounds will be groups to target for early intervention.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>RESEARCH IN CONTEXT</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What is already known about this subject?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>The higher COVID-related death rate in people with diabetes has been well-documented</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>A study involving the residents of Salford, UK showed 135 fewer diagnoses of type 2 diabetes than expected between March and May 2020, which amounted to a 49% reduction in activity</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>There is limited data on the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the diagnosis and monitoring of type 2 diabetes</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What is the key question?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>What has been the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the diagnosis and monitoring of type 2 diabetes across the UK?</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What are the new findings?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>Across the UK, the rate of new type 2 diabetes diagnoses was reduced by up to 70% in April 2020 compared to 10-year historical trends</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>Between March and December 2020, it is estimated that 60,000 people have had a missed or delayed diagnosis</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>
                          The frequency of HbA
                          <jats:sub>1c</jats:sub>
                          monitoring in type 2 diabetes was reduced by 77-83% in April 2020 and by 31-37% overall between March and December 2020
                        </jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>How might this impact on clinical practice in the foreseeable future?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>
                          During this pandemic and associated lockdowns, effective public communications should ensure that patients remain engaged with diabetes services including HbA
                          <jats:sub>1c</jats:sub>
                          screening and monitoring
                        </jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Impact of COVID-19 on diagnoses, monitoring and mortality in people with type 2 diabetes: a UK-wide cohort study involving 14 million people in primary care
